retrieved_docs.content: The different risk factors for phenytoin toxicity are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Phenytoin displays its primary signs of toxicity on the nervous and cardiovascular systems. Overdose of oral phenytoin mainly causes neurotoxicity, whereas cardiovascular toxicity is the main side effect of parenteral administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Neurologist Poison control Cardiologist if the patient has arrhythmias Psychiatry if there was an intentional overdose Dermatologist if the patient has DRESS syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Etiology -- Change in Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Phenytoin toxicity can occur from an increase in the daily dose of phenytoin, changes in the formulations or brands, as well as changes in the frequency of administration. It can also occur when patients are started on new medications that interact with the metabolism or binding capacity of phenytoin to plasma proteins. Due to its metabolism by CYP450 liver enzymes, multiple drug interactions can occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Etiology -- Chronic Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin -- Toxicity -- Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is no specific antidote for phenytoin toxicity; supportive care is the hallmark of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the frequency of phenytoin use. Describe the signs and symptoms of phenytoin toxicity. Outline the evaluation of phenytoin toxicity. Summarize the interprofessional team's coordinated management of phenytoin toxicity and the patient education necessary to avoid toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the 2011 Annual Report of the American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC) National Poison Data System, 1971 single-substance phenytoin exposures resulted in 46 major outcomes and 1 death. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Phenytoin has become one of the most well-studied anticonvulsants. With an average monthly cost of $30, it has also become one of the most widely used anticonvulsants, listed on the World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines. However, with its narrow therapeutic index and its pervasive daily use, considering potential phenytoin overdose or toxicity from chronic use is key to early management and prevention of further toxicity. This activity outlines the medical team's evaluation and management of phenytoin toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the mechanism of action of phenytoin for the proper treatment of seizure disorders. Explain the adverse effects and outline the contraindications of phenytoin therapy. Identify the appropriate methods for therapeutic drug monitoring and select the course of action for phenytoin toxicity. Describe some inter-professional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance phenytoin therapy and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin -- Toxicity -- Other Toxicities
retrieved_docs.content: “Purple glove syndrome” is a rare side effect that can accompany the intravenous administration of phenytoin. The worsening limb edema and discoloration appear to result from the crystallization of phenytoin within the blood. When there is extensive skin necrosis and limb ischemia, this can lead to amputations. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients suspected of possible phenytoin overdose should be evaluated with a broad differential in mind. Fingerstick glucose should be obtained on all patients as well as a pregnancy test on all female patients of child-bearing age. An ECG is recommended for all suspected phenytoin overdose patients, especially patients who have received parenteral phenytoin. Laboratory work should be obtained, including a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), liver function test (LFT), total serum phenytoin concentration, and serum albumin. Urine toxicology, as well as acetaminophen, salicylic acid, and alcohol levels, should also be obtained to complete the toxicologic workup. [1] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Other symptoms of overdose can be managed according to standards of care. Antiemetics can be administered in cases of nausea and vomiting. Seizures can be controlled by following the normal seizure protocols with benzodiazepines as the first medications, followed by phenobarbital or levetiracetam for persistent or recurrent seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Confusion Hallucinations Neuropathy Falls Encephalopathy Urinary incontinence Movement disorders Priapism Rash Hyperglycemia Hypoglycemia

retrieved_docs.title: Phenytoin Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Barbiturate toxicity Benzodiazepine toxicity Carbamazepine toxicity Encephalitis Erythema multiforme Isoniazid toxicity Shock Stevens-johnson syndrome SLE Toxic epidermal necrolysis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a broad spectrum of agents used to treat الصرع (epilepsy), including subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy, and aims to prevent severe neurological sequelae such as coma, stupor, and brain damage. Core EPC members include valproate (and its calcium, sodium, magnesium salts and related derivatives), lamotrigine, tiagabine (including tiagabine hydrochloride), ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin), ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin (fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, and others. These drugs exert anticonvulsant effects through distinct mechanisms, allowing clinicians to tailor seizure control to individual patients and specific seizure types. Polypharmacy considerations are highlighted by observed co‑occurrence patterns: dextropropoxyfen frequently co‑occurs with phenytoin, and phenytoin often co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxycodone, underscoring the need for vigilance regarding drug‑drug interactions. Oxygen deprivation (hypoxia) is closely associated with the same critical outcomes—coma, stupor, and irreversible brain injury—because insufficient cerebral oxygen supply can precipitate a spectrum of unconscious states and permanent neuronal damage. Recognizing the interrelationship between epilepsy, its antiepileptic treatments, hypoxic events, and the potential for severe neurological impairment is essential for individualized therapy, careful monitoring of concomitant medications, and timely intervention to prevent lasting neurological dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: valproate), vigabatrin, and zonisamid are organized as direct children within the class. These agents are selected according to their distinct pharmacologic properties to manage seizures across diverse epilepsy syndromes. Enzyme‑mediated interactions are critical: cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers modify the mechanisms of clobazam and topiramat and are contraindicated with flibanserin (a benzimidazoly‑derived compound), while P‑glycoprotein inhibitors alter the activity of flibanserin and zonisamid. Awareness of CYP3A4‑ and P‑glycoprotein‑mediated drug interactions is essential for optimizing antiepileptic regimens, minimizing adverse effects, and improving patient outcomes. Valproat (valproic acid) poisoning can cause severe neurological injury and present with generalized seizures classified as نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, originating in the cerebrum (velký mozek). Mefobarbital is an effective therapeutic option for controlling these seizures. Hypoxic conditions (عَوَز الأكسجين, oxygen

retrieved_docs.content: Dextropropoxyfen and fenytoin are noted to co‑occur, indicating that these two drugs are often observed together in clinical settings or patient records. This co‑occurrence suggests that patients may be prescribed both medications concurrently, highlighting the importance of monitoring for potential drug‑drug interactions and ensuring coordinated management of conditions treated by dextropropoxyfen and fenytoin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: (topiramat), and many others. These agents are grouped by mechanism: sodium‑channel blockers (phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin sodium, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, zonisamide, lakosamid); GABA‑enhancers (vigabatrin, tiagabine, tiagabine hydrochloride, gabapentin enacarbil); calcium‑channel modulators (lamotrigine, topiramate); and additional pathways targeted by valproic‑acid derivatives, methsuximide, trimethadion, mefenytoin, and primidon. This mechanistic taxonomy enables clinicians to tailor therapy to seizure type, patient comorbidities, and tolerability, thereby optimizing seizure control. Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers modify the pharmacodynamics of the antiepileptics klobazam and topiramate, necessitating dose adjustments or therapeutic drug monitoring to avoid clinically significant interactions. In practice, phenytoin is frequently co‑prescribed with the analgesic dextropropoxyfen and the opioid oxycodone, a polypharmacy pattern that raises concerns about combined toxicity,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC] (anticonvulsants) comprises valproate, valproic acid, calcium valproate, magnesium valproate, sodium valproate, ethotoin, phenytoin, phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, mephobarbital, mefobarbital, lamotrigine, lacosamide, levetiracetam, gabapentin enacarbil, tiagabine, tiagabine hydrochloride, vigabatrin, zonisamide, ethosuximide, methsuximide, trimethadion, primidon, oxcarbazepine, felbamate and other antiepileptics listed under “Other antiepileptics in ATC.” All are indicated for the management of epilepsy and its subtypes, including benign neonatal epilepsy and generalized‑onset non‑motor seizures such as نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة. Specific agents are selected for particular seizure presentations; for example, mephobarbital (mefobarbital) is used for نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة. Epilepsy frequently co‑occurs with headache (صداع) and may be associated with the neuro‑clinical sign تكثر الدبق الدماغي. Toxic exposure to valproate can cause “Poisoning caused by valproate.” Phenytoin (fenytoin) is noted to be co‑prescribed with oxycodone. Brain and nervous system cancer, exemplified by malignant brain tumor (ورم الدماغ الخبيث) located in the velký mozek (brain), is linked to the finding تكثر الدبق الدماغي and may also present with headache. The diagnostic procedure البزل القطني (lumbar puncture) is associated with the development of headache. These interrelated pharmacologic options, clinical manifestations, adverse effects, and anatomical considerations guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic planning in neurological and oncologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: interactions. In practice, phenytoin is frequently co‑prescribed with the analgesic dextropropoxyfen and the opioid oxycodone, a polypharmacy pattern that raises concerns about combined toxicity, altered seizure control, and additive central‑nervous‑system effects. Mefobarbital is employed for generalized‑onset seizures (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة), a seizure type classified under epilepsy ; convulsions and linked to a cerebral origin in the cerebrum (veliký mozek). Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing enzyme‑mediated drug interactions, managing concurrent medications, and understanding the neuroanatomical basis of seizure disorders to ensure safe, personalized antiepileptic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 911
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning caused by valproate is a toxic condition in which the medication valproate acts as the causative agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 27
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class comprises a broad group of agents used to control seizures in epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes, including benign neonatal epilepsy, epilepsy with convulsions, and generalized absence seizures (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة). Core members of this class are the valproate family—valproic acid sodium salt, valproate sodium, valproate calcium (valproát vápenatý), magnesium valproate, the sodium salt of valproic acid, valproát sodný, sodná sůl kyselí valproové, and Valproat—as well as gabapentin, gabapentin enacarbil, tiagabin, tiagabin hydrochlorid, levetiracetam, oxkarbazepin, phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin (including fosphenytoin sodium), phenobarbital‑related compounds (mefenytoin, mefobarbital), ethosuximide, methsuximide, primidon, trimethadion, felbamate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, lakosamide, ethotoin, and mefenytoin. Mefobarbital is specifically noted for treating generalized absence seizures. Valproate toxicity is recognized, with poisoning attributed to Valproat. Gabapentin also has utility in managing anxiety or phobic disorders (رُهاب). Important drug‑interaction considerations include the frequent co‑occurrence of dextropropoxyfen with fenytoin and the interaction between fenytoin and oxycodone, highlighting potential combined exposure risks. Epileptic convulsions are neurological events that typically originate in the cerebrum (velký mozek), and the Arabic‑language classification نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة describes a generalized‑onset seizure with an initial loss of movement. Accurate identification of cerebral localization, seizure type, and the specific antiepileptic agent—along with awareness of toxicity and interaction profiles—facilitates appropriate diagnosis and selection of therapy for patients with diverse epilepsy presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC]” (anticonvulsants) is organized hierarchically to encompass a broad spectrum of agents used for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع). Core members include Valproat (valproic acid and its salts – sodium valproate, magnesium valproate, calcium valproate) together with the child concepts lamotrigine, tiagabine (and tiagabine hydrochloride), and the subclass “Other antiepileptics in ATC”. Additional antiepileptic drugs listed in the class are ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium), mephenytoin, vigabatrin, zonisamide, ethosuximide, felbamate, gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, and clobazam. Benign neonatal epilepsy is defined as a subtype of epilepsy, and epilepsy itself is included in the systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) grouping, reflecting its relevance to autoimmune disease classifications. Several agents—particularly clobazam and topiramate—induce the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme system, and many antiepileptics are metabolized by this pathway. Valproate‑related toxicity is directly attributable to valproate compounds, with Valproat identified as the causative agent in valproate poisoning. In adverse‑event reporting, dextropropoxyfen, phenytoin, and oxycodone are frequently mentioned together, suggesting common use in combination therapy or joint appearance in safety data. Proliferative glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري) is recognized as a renal manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ), underscoring its importance for diagnosis and management of patients with this autoimmune disease. This hierarchy and the pharmacologic, metabolic, safety, and disease‑association relationships among the listed agents support optimal patient management of epilepsy, its specific forms, and related systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a large repertoire of drugs used to manage and prevent seizures in patients with epilepsy (الصرع). Representative agents include lamotrigine, tiagabine and tiagabine hydrochloride, acetazolamide sodium, ethotoin, phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin, valproate calcium (valproát vápenatý), magnesium valproate, zonisamide, ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, and additional agents such as fenytoin, mefenytoin, and lakosamid. These medications act through diverse mechanisms—stabilizing neuronal membranes, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission, or modulating ion channels—to reduce seizure frequency and severity. Epilepsy is associated with serious acute complications, including coma, stupor, and brain damage, which may necessitate respiratory assistance (oxygen support) to prevent further neurological deterioration. A specific subtype, benign neonatal epilepsy, shares the underlying pathophysiology of epilepsy but typically carries a more favorable prognosis. In clinical practice, antiepileptic drugs are often part of polypharmacy regimens. Dextropropoxyfen is frequently co‑administered with fenytoin, and fenytoin is commonly combined with oxycodone to address both seizure control and analgesia. Recognizing these drug‑drug and drug‑condition relationships enables clinicians to tailor comprehensive treatment plans that optimize seizure management, mitigate severe neurological outcomes, and improve long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (anti‑epileptic agents) comprises a broad spectrum of drugs used to control seizure activity in epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy. Core agents include valproate derivatives (valproat, calcium valproate, sodium valproate, magnesium valproate, valproic acid/kyselina valproová, sodium salt of valproic acid, sodná sůl kyseliny valproové), phenobarbital‑related compounds (phenytoin, phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, mefenytoin, ethotoin, felbamát), and newer agents (lamotrigine, tiagabin, tiagabin hydrochloride, levetiracetam, topiramate, oxcarbazepine, zonisamide, vigabatrin, lacosamide/lakosamid, gabapentin enacarbil, ethosuximide, methsuximide, primidone, trimethadione, clobazam). These medications share the primary therapeutic role of seizure control. Clinical observations note a frequent association between epilepsy and headache (صداع), and migraine (شقيقة), a specific form of headache that overlaps with familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية). Prevention of migraine attacks can be achieved with antiepileptic agents, particularly topiramate (topiramat) and valproic acid (kyselina valproová) together with its sodium salt (sodná sůl kyseliny valproové, valproát sodný). Zonisamide acts as a P‑Glycoprotein inhibitor, contributing to its therapeutic effect. The metabolism of clobazam and topiramate is subject to cytochrome P450 3A4 induction, influencing dosing and pharmacodynamics. In practice, dextropropoxyfen is frequently co‑administered with fenytoin, and fenytoin is also commonly combined with oxycodone (oxykodon), reflecting common polypharmacy patterns. Finally, lumbar puncture (البزل القطني, LP) is associated with the development of post‑procedure headache.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class comprises a broad spectrum of agents used to treat epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy. Core members include lamotrigine and tiagabine (tiagabin hydrochloride), with additional agents such as oxcarbazepine (oxkarbazepin), phenytoin (including phenytoin sodium), primidone (primidon), topiramate (topiramat), trimethadione (trimethadion), clobazam (klobazam), methsuximide, vigabatrin, zonisamide (zonisamid), ethosuximide, ethotoin, mefenytoin (mephenytoin), valproate (valproát vápenatý) and its magnesium salt, felbamate (felbamát), fosphenytoin (and fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide (lakosamid), levetiracetam, and isa. These drugs are organized into subclasses such as “Other antiepileptics in ATC,” and include subordinate members like topiramate. Pharmacologically, clobazam and topiramate induce cytochrome P450 3A4 activity, whereas zonisamide inhibits the drug‑transport protein P‑glycoprotein, influencing seizure control and potential drug interactions. Observational data indicate frequent co‑occurrence of dextropropoxyfen with phenytoin and common co‑administration of phenytoin with oxycodone, underscoring the need to consider drug‑drug interactions when selecting antiepileptic regimens. Understanding the classification, mechanisms of action, metabolic pathways, and interaction patterns of these antiepileptic agents is essential for optimizing individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: overlap with the antiepileptic drug zonisamide. Dextropropoxyfen frequently co‑occurs with the anticonvulsant phenytoin, and phenytoin often co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxykodon, illustrating common polypharmacy scenarios. Mefobarbital can be employed to treat the seizure type described as “نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة,” a manifestation within epilepsy and convulsive disorders. Recognizing these drug‑drug interactions and therapeutic links assists clinicians in individualizing seizure therapy, managing concomitant medications, and optimizing patient outcomes.
